added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. This is actually considered an amulet.|website=Tadaima Japan|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ashikariyama.jp/chimaki-e.html|title=About Chimaki|website=Foundation for Ashikari-yama Preservation|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite book |title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London, vol. 12|editor=Japan Society of London|date=1914|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company|pages=69–70|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=B_EVAAAAYAAJ}} A similar custom, involving a ''[[shimenawa]]'' with a wooden plaque on which is written "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon''), is practiced in the [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Shima, Mie|Shima]] areas of [[Mie Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Iseshima Pedia: Guide to Iseshima: Mie Japan|date=2016|publisher=Iseshima Tourism & Convention Organization|page=35|url=https://www.iseshima-kanko.jp/kiji/iseshimapedia/Iseshima_Pedia_lite.pdf}} In [[Ueda, Nagano|Ueda]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], hexagonal wooden charms inscribed with the words "Wealthy [and] prosperous [are] Somin Shōrai's descendant(s)" (大福長者蘇民将来子孫人也, ''Daifuku chōja Somin Shōrai shison no hito nari'') traditionally handmade by members of the local Somin Confraternity (蘇民講 ''Somin-kō'') are distributed by [[Shinano Kokubun-ji]] temple during the Yōkadō Festival (八日堂縁日 ''Yōkadō Ennichi'') held every 7th-8 January.{{cite web|url=http://bunkashisan.ne.jp/search/ViewContent_e.php?from=10&ContentID=262|title=Somin Shorai-fu or Somin Shorai talisman - Our wish is forever and ever - Folkways-cultural record of the distribution of Somin Shorai-fu of Yokado, Ueda City|website=Regional Cultural Asset Portal|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://shinano-kokubunji.or.jp/youkadou.html|title=八日堂縁日について|website=Shinano Kokubun-ji|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://db.umic.ueda.nagano.jp/johogura/kangawa/0527045/0527045e.html|title=Somin Shorai Talisman Makers Shinano Kokubunji Temple|website= Ueda City Multimedia Information Center|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==See also== {{Portal|Mythology|Religion|Philosophy}} * [[Baucis and Philemon]] * [[Folk medicine]] * [[Harae]] * [[Omamori]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Sodom and Gomorrah]] * [[Susanoo]] * [[Xenia (Greek)|Theoxenia]] ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Somin Shourai}} *[http://live.ueda.ne.jp/kokubu.html Shinano Kokubunji Temple] [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Woodcarving]] [[Category:Onmyōdō deities]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Gion cult]] {{Gion cult}} [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]]

{{short description|Figure in Japanese folklore}} In [[Japanese mythology]] and [[Japanese folklore|folklore]], '''Somin Shōrai''' (蘇民将来, ''[[kyūjitai]]'': 蘇民將來; also [[Variant Chinese character|written as]] 蘓民將耒) was a poor man who gave food and shelter to a certain [[Kami|god]] in the guise of a traveler who was looking for a place to stay. As a reward, the god provided Somin Shōrai's family a means to save themselves from an oncoming pestilence that eventually claimed the lives of those who had turned him away earlier. The story of Somin Shōrai is the basis for the [[Shinto]] custom of walking through a large ring of twisted [[miscanthus]] reeds during the beginning of summer at many [[Shinto shrine]]s across Japan. [[Omamori|Talismans]] bearing Somin Shōrai's name are also popularly held to ward off disease and misfortune. ==Legend== [[File:Meiji standing screen from Crow Collection 02.jpg|thumb|Mutō-no-Kami (武塔神) or Mutō Tenjin (武塔天神), the god who received hospitality from Somin Shōrai, is identified with [[Susanoo]] (pictured)]] The earliest version of the Somin Shōrai legend is found in the imperially commissioned gazetteer (''[[Fudoki]]'') of [[Bingo Province]] (modern eastern [[Hiroshima Prefecture]]) compiled during the [[Nara period]], surviving in an extract quoted by medieval scholar and Shinto priest Urabe Kanekata in the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. [[File:Gozu-Tenno.jpg|thumb|left|Gozu Tennō, a [[Shinbutsu shūgō|syncretic]] deity associated with plague and disease historically conflated with Susanoo and Mutō-no-Kami]] According to the story, a god from the northern sea named, ''Mutō-no-Kami'' (武塔神), who was embarking on a long journey to court the daughter of his counterpart, the god of the southern sea, asked for a place to stay for the night from two brothers. While the wealthy, younger brother refused the deity any hospitality, the elder brother, named ''Somin Shōrai'', though extremely poor, took him in as a guest and shared all that he had: a bed of [[Foxtail millet|millet]]-straw and a simple meal of cooked millet seeds. Years later, ''Mutō'' (who had since married the southern sea god's daughter and had eight children with her) returned to ''Somin Shōrai's'' old house and gave to ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter a [[wreath]] of entwined [[miscanthus]] reeds (a [[Shimenawa]] wreath) as a reward for the hospitality shown to him by her late father, telling her to wear it around her waist from then-on. At that same night, the god exterminated all the inhabitants of the area, sparing only ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter. ''Mutō'' then revealed himself to have actually been the god, ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'', and promised to her that, in the future, ''all'' those who would wear miscanthus wreaths around their waists while declaring themselves to be ''Somin Shōrai's'' descendants, in times of epidemics, would remain unharmed.{{cite book |editor1-last=Akimoto |editor1-first=Kichirō |title=日本古典文学大系 2 風土記 (Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei, 2: Fudoki) |date=1958 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=488–489}}{{cite journal |last1=McMullin |first1=Neil |title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult |journal=History of Religions |date=February 1988 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=270–293 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press|doi=10.1086/463123 |jstor=1062279 |s2cid=162357693 }} In some later versions of the legend, the rich younger brother is given the name ''Kotan Shōrai'' (巨旦将来), while the god who stayed in ''Somin Shōrai's'' house is identified, instead of Susanoo, as ''[[Gozu Tennō]]'' (牛頭天王 'Ox-Headed Heavenly King'), a pestilence deity conflated with both ''Mutō'' and ''Susanoo'' (though one version instead identifies ''Gozu Tennō'' as ''Mutō's'' son).{{cite book |title=The Japan Mail |date=1878-03-11 |publisher=Jappan Mēru Shinbunsha |pages=138–139 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IhJCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA169}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |page=181 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA181}}{{cite web |title=牛頭天王と蘇民将来 (Gozu Tennō to Somin Shōrai) |url=http://www.futami-somin.com/somin.htm |website=民話の駅 蘇民 (Minwa no Eki Somin) |access-date=2020-06-17 |language=ja}} In the version of the story found in the ''[[:ja:三国相伝陰陽カン轄ホキ内伝金烏玉兎集|Hoki Naiden]]'', an ''[[Onmyōdō]]'' text on divination attributed to ''[[Abe no Seimei]]'', ''Kotan Shōrai'' (portrayed here as the king of an Indian kingdom), who was slain by ''Gozu Tennō'' as punishment for his lack of generosity, is identified with the evil deity, ''[[Konjin]]''. In other variants, the divine traveller gives ''Somin Shōrai's'' family a talisman with the inscription "[I am] the descendant of ''Somin Shōrai''" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') as protection against pestilence. ==''Chi-no-wa kuguri''== [[File:Haiden of Kamado Shrine.jpg|thumb|290px|A ''chi-no-wa'' erected in front of a Shinto shrine]] The legend forms the basis for the ''chi-no-wa kuguri'' (茅の輪くぐり) ritual performed in many [[Shinto shrines]] mainly during the annual Summer Purification Ritual (夏越の祓 ''Nagoshi no [[Harae]]'' or 夏越の大祓 ''Nagoshi no Ōharae'') held at the end of June. This rite involves passing through a large miscanthus hoop (茅の輪 ''chi-no-wa'') set up at the shrine's entrance or within its precincts, usually while reciting one or more seasonal ''[[Waka (poetry)|waka]]'' such as the one below followed by a double recitation of Somin Shōrai's name. Walking through this ring is believed to cleanse sins and other defilements (''[[kegare]]'') and guard against misfortune.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Nagoshi no harae |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1064 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |last1=Motosawa |first1=Masashi |title=Chinowa |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=301 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=Nagoshi Gohan |url=https://kome-academy.com/en/teishoku_library/season/2018_summer.html |website=Plenus "kome" Academy |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=「茅の輪くぐり」唱え詞 |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/%E3%80%8C%E8%8C%85%E3%81%AE%E8%BC%AA%E3%81%8F%E3%81%90%E3%82%8A%E3%80%8D%E5%94%B1%E3%81%88%E8%A9%9E/ |website=諫早神社 (Isahaya Shrine) |access-date=2020-06-20 |language=ja}} {{Verse translation | lang = Japanese | italicsoff = 1 | rtl1 = |水無月の (''Minazuki no'')
夏越の祓 (''Nagoshi no harae'')
する人は (''suru hito wa'')
千歳の命 (''chitose no inochi'')
延ぶといふなり (''nobu to iu nari'') |In [[Japanese calendar#Months|Minazuki]],
at the passing of summer,
those who purify themselves
are said to have a thousand years
added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. This is actually considered an amulet.|website=Tadaima Japan|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ashikariyama.jp/chimaki-e.html|title=About Chimaki|website=Foundation for Ashikari-yama Preservation|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite book |title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London, vol. 12|editor=Japan Society of London|date=1914|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company|pages=69–70|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=B_EVAAAAYAAJ}} A similar custom, involving a ''[[shimenawa]]'' with a wooden plaque on which is written "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon''), is practiced in the [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Shima, Mie|Shima]] areas of [[Mie Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Iseshima Pedia: Guide to Iseshima: Mie Japan|date=2016|publisher=Iseshima Tourism & Convention Organization|page=35|url=https://www.iseshima-kanko.jp/kiji/iseshimapedia/Iseshima_Pedia_lite.pdf}} In [[Ueda, Nagano|Ueda]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], hexagonal wooden charms inscribed with the words "Wealthy [and] prosperous [are] Somin Shōrai's descendant(s)" (大福長者蘇民将来子孫人也, ''Daifuku chōja Somin Shōrai shison no hito nari'') traditionally handmade by members of the local Somin Confraternity (蘇民講 ''Somin-kō'') are distributed by [[Shinano Kokubun-ji]] temple during the Yōkadō Festival (八日堂縁日 ''Yōkadō Ennichi'') held every 7th-8 January.{{cite web|url=http://bunkashisan.ne.jp/search/ViewContent_e.php?from=10&ContentID=262|title=Somin Shorai-fu or Somin Shorai talisman - Our wish is forever and ever - Folkways-cultural record of the distribution of Somin Shorai-fu of Yokado, Ueda City|website=Regional Cultural Asset Portal|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://shinano-kokubunji.or.jp/youkadou.html|title=八日堂縁日について|website=Shinano Kokubun-ji|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://db.umic.ueda.nagano.jp/johogura/kangawa/0527045/0527045e.html|title=Somin Shorai Talisman Makers Shinano Kokubunji Temple|website= Ueda City Multimedia Information Center|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==See also== {{Portal|Mythology|Religion|Philosophy}} * [[Baucis and Philemon]] * [[Folk medicine]] * [[Harae]] * [[Omamori]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Sodom and Gomorrah]] * [[Susanoo]] * [[Xenia (Greek)|Theoxenia]] ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Somin Shourai}} *[http://live.ueda.ne.jp/kokubu.html Shinano Kokubunji Temple] [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Woodcarving]] [[Category:Onmyōdō deities]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Gion cult]] {{Gion cult}} [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]]

This rite involves passing through a large miscanthus hoop (茅の輪 ''chi-no-wa'') set up at the shrine's entrance or within its precincts, usually while reciting one or more seasonal ''[[Waka (poetry)|waka]]'' such as the one below followed by a double recitation of Somin Shōrai's name. Walking through this ring is believed to cleanse sins and other defilements (''[[kegare]]'') and guard against misfortune.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Nagoshi no harae |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1064 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |last1=Motosawa |first1=Masashi |title=Chinowa |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=301 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=Nagoshi Gohan |url=https://kome-academy.com/en/teishoku_library/season/2018_summer.html |website=Plenus "kome" Academy |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=「茅の輪くぐり」唱え詞 |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/%E3%80%8C%E8%8C%85%E3%81%AE%E8%BC%AA%E3%81%8F%E3%81%90%E3%82%8A%E3%80%8D%E5%94%B1%E3%81%88%E8%A9%9E/ |website=諫早神社 (Isahaya Shrine) |access-date=2020-06-20 |language=ja}} {{Verse translation | lang = Japanese | italicsoff = 1 | rtl1 = |水無月の (''Minazuki no'')
夏越の祓 (''Nagoshi no harae'')
する人は (''suru hito wa'')
千歳の命 (''chitose no inochi'')
延ぶといふなり (''nobu to iu nari'') |In [[Japanese calendar#Months|Minazuki]],
at the passing of summer,
those who purify themselves
are said to have a thousand years
added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. This is actually considered an amulet.|website=Tadaima Japan|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ashikariyama.jp/chimaki-e.html|title=About Chimaki|website=Foundation for Ashikari-yama Preservation|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite book |title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London, vol. 12|editor=Japan Society of London|date=1914|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company|pages=69–70|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=B_EVAAAAYAAJ}} A similar custom, involving a ''[[shimenawa]]'' with a wooden plaque on which is written "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon''), is practiced in the [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Shima, Mie|Shima]] areas of [[Mie Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Iseshima Pedia: Guide to Iseshima: Mie Japan|date=2016|publisher=Iseshima Tourism & Convention Organization|page=35|url=https://www.iseshima-kanko.jp/kiji/iseshimapedia/Iseshima_Pedia_lite.pdf}} In [[Ueda, Nagano|Ueda]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], hexagonal wooden charms inscribed with the words "Wealthy [and] prosperous [are] Somin Shōrai's descendant(s)" (大福長者蘇民将来子孫人也, ''Daifuku chōja Somin Shōrai shison no hito nari'') traditionally handmade by members of the local Somin Confraternity (蘇民講 ''Somin-kō'') are distributed by [[Shinano Kokubun-ji]] temple during the Yōkadō Festival (八日堂縁日 ''Yōkadō Ennichi'') held every 7th-8 January.{{cite web|url=http://bunkashisan.ne.jp/search/ViewContent_e.php?from=10&ContentID=262|title=Somin Shorai-fu or Somin Shorai talisman - Our wish is forever and ever - Folkways-cultural record of the distribution of Somin Shorai-fu of Yokado, Ueda City|website=Regional Cultural Asset Portal|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://shinano-kokubunji.or.jp/youkadou.html|title=八日堂縁日について|website=Shinano Kokubun-ji|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://db.umic.ueda.nagano.jp/johogura/kangawa/0527045/0527045e.html|title=Somin Shorai Talisman Makers Shinano Kokubunji Temple|website= Ueda City Multimedia Information Center|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==See also== {{Portal|Mythology|Religion|Philosophy}} * [[Baucis and Philemon]] * [[Folk medicine]] * [[Harae]] * [[Omamori]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Sodom and Gomorrah]] * [[Susanoo]] * [[Xenia (Greek)|Theoxenia]] ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Somin Shourai}} *[http://live.ueda.ne.jp/kokubu.html Shinano Kokubunji Temple] [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Woodcarving]] [[Category:Onmyōdō deities]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Gion cult]] {{Gion cult}} [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Figure in Japanese folklore}} In [[Japanese mythology]] and [[Japanese folklore|folklore]], '''Somin Shōrai''' (蘇民将来, ''[[kyūjitai]]'': 蘇民將來; also [[Variant Chinese character|written as]] 蘓民將耒) was a poor man who gave food and shelter to a certain [[Kami|god]] in the guise of a traveler who was looking for a place to stay. As a reward, the god provided Somin Shōrai's family a means to save themselves from an oncoming pestilence that eventually claimed the lives of those who had turned him away earlier. The story of Somin Shōrai is the basis for the [[Shinto]] custom of walking through a large ring of twisted [[miscanthus]] reeds during the beginning of summer at many [[Shinto shrine]]s across Japan. [[Omamori|Talismans]] bearing Somin Shōrai's name are also popularly held to ward off disease and misfortune. ==Legend== [[File:Meiji standing screen from Crow Collection 02.jpg|thumb|Mutō-no-Kami (武塔神) or Mutō Tenjin (武塔天神), the god who received hospitality from Somin Shōrai, is identified with [[Susanoo]] (pictured)]] The earliest version of the Somin Shōrai legend is found in the imperially commissioned gazetteer (''[[Fudoki]]'') of [[Bingo Province]] (modern eastern [[Hiroshima Prefecture]]) compiled during the [[Nara period]], surviving in an extract quoted by medieval scholar and Shinto priest Urabe Kanekata in the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. [[File:Gozu-Tenno.jpg|thumb|left|Gozu Tennō, a [[Shinbutsu shūgō|syncretic]] deity associated with plague and disease historically conflated with Susanoo and Mutō-no-Kami]] According to the story, a god from the northern sea named, ''Mutō-no-Kami'' (武塔神), who was embarking on a long journey to court the daughter of his counterpart, the god of the southern sea, asked for a place to stay for the night from two brothers. While the wealthy, younger brother refused the deity any hospitality, the elder brother, named ''Somin Shōrai'', though extremely poor, took him in as a guest and shared all that he had: a bed of [[Foxtail millet|millet]]-straw and a simple meal of cooked millet seeds. Years later, ''Mutō'' (who had since married the southern sea god's daughter and had eight children with her) returned to ''Somin Shōrai's'' old house and gave to ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter a [[wreath]] of entwined [[miscanthus]] reeds (a [[Shimenawa]] wreath) as a reward for the hospitality shown to him by her late father, telling her to wear it around her waist from then-on. At that same night, the god exterminated all the inhabitants of the area, sparing only ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter. ''Mutō'' then revealed himself to have actually been the god, ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'', and promised to her that, in the future, ''all'' those who would wear miscanthus wreaths around their waists while declaring themselves to be ''Somin Shōrai's'' descendants, in times of epidemics, would remain unharmed.{{cite book |editor1-last=Akimoto |editor1-first=Kichirō |title=日本古典文学大系 2 風土記 (Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei, 2: Fudoki) |date=1958 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=488–489}}{{cite journal |last1=McMullin |first1=Neil |title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult |journal=History of Religions |date=February 1988 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=270–293 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press|doi=10.1086/463123 |jstor=1062279 |s2cid=162357693 }} In some later versions of the legend, the rich younger brother is given the name ''Kotan Shōrai'' (巨旦将来), while the god who stayed in ''Somin Shōrai's'' house is identified, instead of Susanoo, as ''[[Gozu Tennō]]'' (牛頭天王 'Ox-Headed Heavenly King'), a pestilence deity conflated with both ''Mutō'' and ''Susanoo'' (though one version instead identifies ''Gozu Tennō'' as ''Mutō's'' son).{{cite book |title=The Japan Mail |date=1878-03-11 |publisher=Jappan Mēru Shinbunsha |pages=138–139 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IhJCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA169}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |page=181 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA181}}{{cite web |title=牛頭天王と蘇民将来 (Gozu Tennō to Somin Shōrai) |url=http://www.futami-somin.com/somin.htm |website=民話の駅 蘇民 (Minwa no Eki Somin) |access-date=2020-06-17 |language=ja}} In the version of the story found in the ''[[:ja:三国相伝陰陽カン轄ホキ内伝金烏玉兎集|Hoki Naiden]]'', an ''[[Onmyōdō]]'' text on divination attributed to ''[[Abe no Seimei]]'', ''Kotan Shōrai'' (portrayed here as the king of an Indian kingdom), who was slain by ''Gozu Tennō'' as punishment for his lack of generosity, is identified with the evil deity, ''[[Konjin]]''. In other variants, the divine traveller gives ''Somin Shōrai's'' family a talisman with the inscription "[I am] the descendant of ''Somin Shōrai''" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') as protection against pestilence. ==''Chi-no-wa kuguri''== [[File:Haiden of Kamado Shrine.jpg|thumb|290px|A ''chi-no-wa'' erected in front of a Shinto shrine]] The legend forms the basis for the ''chi-no-wa kuguri'' (茅の輪くぐり) ritual performed in many [[Shinto shrines]] mainly during the annual Summer Purification Ritual (夏越の祓 ''Nagoshi no [[Harae]]'' or 夏越の大祓 ''Nagoshi no Ōharae'') held at the end of June. This rite involves passing through a large miscanthus hoop (茅の輪 ''chi-no-wa'') set up at the shrine's entrance or within its precincts, usually while reciting one or more seasonal ''[[Waka (poetry)|waka]]'' such as the one below followed by a double recitation of Somin Shōrai's name. Walking through this ring is believed to cleanse sins and other defilements (''[[kegare]]'') and guard against misfortune.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Nagoshi no harae |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1064 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |last1=Motosawa |first1=Masashi |title=Chinowa |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=301 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=Nagoshi Gohan |url=https://kome-academy.com/en/teishoku_library/season/2018_summer.html |website=Plenus "kome" Academy |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=「茅の輪くぐり」唱え詞 |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/%E3%80%8C%E8%8C%85%E3%81%AE%E8%BC%AA%E3%81%8F%E3%81%90%E3%82%8A%E3%80%8D%E5%94%B1%E3%81%88%E8%A9%9E/ |website=諫早神社 (Isahaya Shrine) |access-date=2020-06-20 |language=ja}} {{Verse translation | lang = Japanese | italicsoff = 1 | rtl1 = |水無月の (''Minazuki no'')
夏越の祓 (''Nagoshi no harae'')
する人は (''suru hito wa'')
千歳の命 (''chitose no inochi'')
延ぶといふなり (''nobu to iu nari'') |In [[Japanese calendar#Months|Minazuki]],
at the passing of summer,
those who purify themselves
are said to have a thousand years
added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. (Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei, 2: Fudoki) |date=1958 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=488–489}}{{cite journal |last1=McMullin |first1=Neil |title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult |journal=History of Religions |date=February 1988 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=270–293 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press|doi=10.1086/463123 |jstor=1062279 |s2cid=162357693 }} In some later versions of the legend, the rich younger brother is given the name ''Kotan Shōrai'' (巨旦将来), while the god who stayed in ''Somin Shōrai's'' house is identified, instead of Susanoo, as ''[[Gozu Tennō]]'' (牛頭天王 'Ox-Headed Heavenly King'), a pestilence deity conflated with both ''Mutō'' and ''Susanoo'' (though one version instead identifies ''Gozu Tennō'' as ''Mutō's'' son).{{cite book |title=The Japan Mail |date=1878-03-11 |publisher=Jappan Mēru Shinbunsha |pages=138–139 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IhJCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA169}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |page=181 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA181}}{{cite web |title=牛頭天王と蘇民将来 (Gozu Tennō to Somin Shōrai) |url=http://www.futami-somin.com/somin.htm |website=民話の駅 蘇民 (Minwa no Eki Somin) |access-date=2020-06-17 |language=ja}} In the version of the story found in the ''[[:ja:三国相伝陰陽カン轄ホキ内伝金烏玉兎集|Hoki Naiden]]'', an ''[[Onmyōdō]]'' text on divination attributed to ''[[Abe no Seimei]]'', ''Kotan Shōrai'' (portrayed here as the king of an Indian kingdom), who was slain by ''Gozu Tennō'' as punishment for his lack of generosity, is identified with the evil deity, ''[[Konjin]]''. In other variants, the divine traveller gives ''Somin Shōrai's'' family a talisman with the inscription "[I am] the descendant of ''Somin Shōrai''" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') as protection against pestilence. ==''Chi-no-wa kuguri''== [[File:Haiden of Kamado Shrine.jpg|thumb|290px|A ''chi-no-wa'' erected in front of a Shinto shrine]] The legend forms the basis for the ''chi-no-wa kuguri'' (茅の輪くぐり) ritual performed in many [[Shinto shrines]] mainly during the annual Summer Purification Ritual (夏越の祓 ''Nagoshi no [[Harae]]'' or 夏越の大祓 ''Nagoshi no Ōharae'') held at the end of June. This rite involves passing through a large miscanthus hoop (茅の輪 ''chi-no-wa'') set up at the shrine's entrance or within its precincts, usually while reciting one or more seasonal ''[[Waka (poetry)|waka]]'' such as the one below followed by a double recitation of Somin Shōrai's name. Walking through this ring is believed to cleanse sins and other defilements (''[[kegare]]'') and guard against misfortune.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Nagoshi no harae |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1064 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |last1=Motosawa |first1=Masashi |title=Chinowa |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=301 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=Nagoshi Gohan |url=https://kome-academy.com/en/teishoku_library/season/2018_summer.html |website=Plenus "kome" Academy |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=「茅の輪くぐり」唱え詞 |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/%E3%80%8C%E8%8C%85%E3%81%AE%E8%BC%AA%E3%81%8F%E3%81%90%E3%82%8A%E3%80%8D%E5%94%B1%E3%81%88%E8%A9%9E/ |website=諫早神社 (Isahaya Shrine) |access-date=2020-06-20 |language=ja}} {{Verse translation | lang = Japanese | italicsoff = 1 | rtl1 = |水無月の (''Minazuki no'')
夏越の祓 (''Nagoshi no harae'')
する人は (''suru hito wa'')
千歳の命 (''chitose no inochi'')
延ぶといふなり (''nobu to iu nari'') |In [[Japanese calendar#Months|Minazuki]],
at the passing of summer,
those who purify themselves
are said to have a thousand years
added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. This is actually considered an amulet.|website=Tadaima Japan|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ashikariyama.jp/chimaki-e.html|title=About Chimaki|website=Foundation for Ashikari-yama Preservation|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite book |title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London, vol. 12|editor=Japan Society of London|date=1914|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company|pages=69–70|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=B_EVAAAAYAAJ}} A similar custom, involving a ''[[shimenawa]]'' with a wooden plaque on which is written "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon''), is practiced in the [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Shima, Mie|Shima]] areas of [[Mie Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Iseshima Pedia: Guide to Iseshima: Mie Japan|date=2016|publisher=Iseshima Tourism & Convention Organization|page=35|url=https://www.iseshima-kanko.jp/kiji/iseshimapedia/Iseshima_Pedia_lite.pdf}} In [[Ueda, Nagano|Ueda]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], hexagonal wooden charms inscribed with the words "Wealthy [and] prosperous [are] Somin Shōrai's descendant(s)" (大福長者蘇民将来子孫人也, ''Daifuku chōja Somin Shōrai shison no hito nari'') traditionally handmade by members of the local Somin Confraternity (蘇民講 ''Somin-kō'') are distributed by [[Shinano Kokubun-ji]] temple during the Yōkadō Festival (八日堂縁日 ''Yōkadō Ennichi'') held every 7th-8 January.{{cite web|url=http://bunkashisan.ne.jp/search/ViewContent_e.php?from=10&ContentID=262|title=Somin Shorai-fu or Somin Shorai talisman - Our wish is forever and ever - Folkways-cultural record of the distribution of Somin Shorai-fu of Yokado, Ueda City|website=Regional Cultural Asset Portal|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://shinano-kokubunji.or.jp/youkadou.html|title=八日堂縁日について|website=Shinano Kokubun-ji|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://db.umic.ueda.nagano.jp/johogura/kangawa/0527045/0527045e.html|title=Somin Shorai Talisman Makers Shinano Kokubunji Temple|website= Ueda City Multimedia Information Center|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==See also== {{Portal|Mythology|Religion|Philosophy}} * [[Baucis and Philemon]] * [[Folk medicine]] * [[Harae]] * [[Omamori]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Sodom and Gomorrah]] * [[Susanoo]] * [[Xenia (Greek)|Theoxenia]] ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Somin Shourai}} *[http://live.ueda.ne.jp/kokubu.html Shinano Kokubunji Temple] [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Woodcarving]] [[Category:Onmyōdō deities]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Gion cult]] {{Gion cult}} [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Somin Shōrai}}{{citation needed|Somin Shōrai}}

{{short description|Figure in Japanese folklore}} In [[Japanese mythology]] and [[Japanese folklore|folklore]], '''Somin Shōrai''' (蘇民将来, ''[[kyūjitai]]'': 蘇民將來; also [[Variant Chinese character|written as]] 蘓民將耒) was a poor man who gave food and shelter to a certain [[Kami|god]] in the guise of a traveler who was looking for a place to stay. As a reward, the god provided Somin Shōrai's family a means to save themselves from an oncoming pestilence that eventually claimed the lives of those who had turned him away earlier. The story of Somin Shōrai is the basis for the [[Shinto]] custom of walking through a large ring of twisted [[miscanthus]] reeds during the beginning of summer at many [[Shinto shrine]]s across Japan. [[Omamori|Talismans]] bearing Somin Shōrai's name are also popularly held to ward off disease and misfortune. ==Legend== [[File:Meiji standing screen from Crow Collection 02.jpg|thumb|Mutō-no-Kami (武塔神) or Mutō Tenjin (武塔天神), the god who received hospitality from Somin Shōrai, is identified with [[Susanoo]] (pictured)]] The earliest version of the Somin Shōrai legend is found in the imperially commissioned gazetteer (''[[Fudoki]]'') of [[Bingo Province]] (modern eastern [[Hiroshima Prefecture]]) compiled during the [[Nara period]], surviving in an extract quoted by medieval scholar and Shinto priest Urabe Kanekata in the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. [[File:Gozu-Tenno.jpg|thumb|left|Gozu Tennō, a [[Shinbutsu shūgō|syncretic]] deity associated with plague and disease historically conflated with Susanoo and Mutō-no-Kami]] According to the story, a god from the northern sea named, ''Mutō-no-Kami'' (武塔神), who was embarking on a long journey to court the daughter of his counterpart, the god of the southern sea, asked for a place to stay for the night from two brothers. While the wealthy, younger brother refused the deity any hospitality, the elder brother, named ''Somin Shōrai'', though extremely poor, took him in as a guest and shared all that he had: a bed of [[Foxtail millet|millet]]-straw and a simple meal of cooked millet seeds. Years later, ''Mutō'' (who had since married the southern sea god's daughter and had eight children with her) returned to ''Somin Shōrai's'' old house and gave to ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter a [[wreath]] of entwined [[miscanthus]] reeds (a [[Shimenawa]] wreath) as a reward for the hospitality shown to him by her late father, telling her to wear it around her waist from then-on. At that same night, the god exterminated all the inhabitants of the area, sparing only ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter. ''Mutō'' then revealed himself to have actually been the god, ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'', and promised to her that, in the future, ''all'' those who would wear miscanthus wreaths around their waists while declaring themselves to be ''Somin Shōrai's'' descendants, in times of epidemics, would remain unharmed.{{cite book |editor1-last=Akimoto |editor1-first=Kichirō |title=日本古典文学大系 2 風土記 (Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei, 2: Fudoki) |date=1958 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=488–489}}{{cite journal |last1=McMullin |first1=Neil |title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult |journal=History of Religions |date=February 1988 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=270–293 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press|doi=10.1086/463123 |jstor=1062279 |s2cid=162357693 }} In some later versions of the legend, the rich younger brother is given the name ''Kotan Shōrai'' (巨旦将来), while the god who stayed in ''Somin Shōrai's'' house is identified, instead of Susanoo, as ''[[Gozu Tennō]]'' (牛頭天王 'Ox-Headed Heavenly King'), a pestilence deity conflated with both ''Mutō'' and ''Susanoo'' (though one version instead identifies ''Gozu Tennō'' as ''Mutō's'' son).{{cite book |title=The Japan Mail |date=1878-03-11 |publisher=Jappan Mēru Shinbunsha |pages=138–139 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IhJCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA169}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |page=181 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA181}}{{cite web |title=牛頭天王と蘇民将来 (Gozu Tennō to Somin Shōrai) |url=http://www.futami-somin.com/somin.htm |website=民話の駅 蘇民 (Minwa no Eki Somin) |access-date=2020-06-17 |language=ja}} In the version of the story found in the ''[[:ja:三国相伝陰陽カン轄ホキ内伝金烏玉兎集|Hoki Naiden]]'', an ''[[Onmyōdō]]'' text on divination attributed to ''[[Abe no Seimei]]'', ''Kotan Shōrai'' (portrayed here as the king of an Indian kingdom), who was slain by ''Gozu Tennō'' as punishment for his lack of generosity, is identified with the evil deity, ''[[Konjin]]''. In other variants, the divine traveller gives ''Somin Shōrai's'' family a talisman with the inscription "[I am] the descendant of ''Somin Shōrai''" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') as protection against pestilence. ==''Chi-no-wa kuguri''== [[File:Haiden of Kamado Shrine.jpg|thumb|290px|A ''chi-no-wa'' erected in front of a Shinto shrine]] The legend forms the basis for the ''chi-no-wa kuguri'' (茅の輪くぐり) ritual performed in many [[Shinto shrines]] mainly during the annual Summer Purification Ritual (夏越の祓 ''Nagoshi no [[Harae]]'' or 夏越の大祓 ''Nagoshi no Ōharae'') held at the end of June. This rite involves passing through a large miscanthus hoop (茅の輪 ''chi-no-wa'') set up at the shrine's entrance or within its precincts, usually while reciting one or more seasonal ''[[Waka (poetry)|waka]]'' such as the one below followed by a double recitation of Somin Shōrai's name. Walking through this ring is believed to cleanse sins and other defilements (''[[kegare]]'') and guard against misfortune.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Nagoshi no harae |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1064 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |last1=Motosawa |first1=Masashi |title=Chinowa |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=301 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=Nagoshi Gohan |url=https://kome-academy.com/en/teishoku_library/season/2018_summer.html |website=Plenus "kome" Academy |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=「茅の輪くぐり」唱え詞 |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/%E3%80%8C%E8%8C%85%E3%81%AE%E8%BC%AA%E3%81%8F%E3%81%90%E3%82%8A%E3%80%8D%E5%94%B1%E3%81%88%E8%A9%9E/ |website=諫早神社 (Isahaya Shrine) |access-date=2020-06-20 |language=ja}} {{Verse translation | lang = Japanese | italicsoff = 1 | rtl1 = |水無月の (''Minazuki no'')
夏越の祓 (''Nagoshi no harae'')
する人は (''suru hito wa'')
千歳の命 (''chitose no inochi'')
延ぶといふなり (''nobu to iu nari'') |In [[Japanese calendar#Months|Minazuki]],
at the passing of summer,
those who purify themselves
are said to have a thousand years
added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. This is actually considered an amulet.|website=Tadaima Japan|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ashikariyama.jp/chimaki-e.html|title=About Chimaki|website=Foundation for Ashikari-yama Preservation|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite book |title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London, vol. 12|editor=Japan Society of London|date=1914|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company|pages=69–70|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=B_EVAAAAYAAJ}} A similar custom, involving a ''[[shimenawa]]'' with a wooden plaque on which is written "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon''), is practiced in the [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Shima, Mie|Shima]] areas of [[Mie Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Iseshima Pedia: Guide to Iseshima: Mie Japan|date=2016|publisher=Iseshima Tourism & Convention Organization|page=35|url=https://www.iseshima-kanko.jp/kiji/iseshimapedia/Iseshima_Pedia_lite.pdf}} In [[Ueda, Nagano|Ueda]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], hexagonal wooden charms inscribed with the words "Wealthy [and] prosperous [are] Somin Shōrai's descendant(s)" (大福長者蘇民将来子孫人也, ''Daifuku chōja Somin Shōrai shison no hito nari'') traditionally handmade by members of the local Somin Confraternity (蘇民講 ''Somin-kō'') are distributed by [[Shinano Kokubun-ji]] temple during the Yōkadō Festival (八日堂縁日 ''Yōkadō Ennichi'') held every 7th-8 January.{{cite web|url=http://bunkashisan.ne.jp/search/ViewContent_e.php?from=10&ContentID=262|title=Somin Shorai-fu or Somin Shorai talisman - Our wish is forever and ever - Folkways-cultural record of the distribution of Somin Shorai-fu of Yokado, Ueda City|website=Regional Cultural Asset Portal|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://shinano-kokubunji.or.jp/youkadou.html|title=八日堂縁日について|website=Shinano Kokubun-ji|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://db.umic.ueda.nagano.jp/johogura/kangawa/0527045/0527045e.html|title=Somin Shorai Talisman Makers Shinano Kokubunji Temple|website= Ueda City Multimedia Information Center|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==See also== {{Portal|Mythology|Religion|Philosophy}} * [[Baucis and Philemon]] * [[Folk medicine]] * [[Harae]] * [[Omamori]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Sodom and Gomorrah]] * [[Susanoo]] * [[Xenia (Greek)|Theoxenia]] ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Somin Shourai}} *[http://live.ueda.ne.jp/kokubu.html Shinano Kokubunji Temple] {{Gion cult}} {{citation needed|Somin Shōrai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Woodcarving]] [[Category:Onmyōdō deities]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Somin Shōrai}}

{{short description|Figure in Japanese folklore}} In [[Japanese mythology]] and [[Japanese folklore|folklore]], '''Somin Shōrai''' (蘇民将来, ''[[kyūjitai]]'': 蘇民將來; also [[Variant Chinese character|written as]] 蘓民將耒) was a poor man who gave food and shelter to a certain [[Kami|god]] in the guise of a traveler who was looking for a place to stay. As a reward, the god provided Somin Shōrai's family a means to save themselves from an oncoming pestilence that eventually claimed the lives of those who had turned him away earlier. The story of Somin Shōrai is the basis for the [[Shinto]] custom of walking through a large ring of twisted [[miscanthus]] reeds during the beginning of summer at many [[Shinto shrine]]s across Japan. [[Omamori|Talismans]] bearing Somin Shōrai's name are also popularly held to ward off disease and misfortune. ==Legend== [[File:Meiji standing screen from Crow Collection 02.jpg|thumb|Mutō-no-Kami (武塔神) or Mutō Tenjin (武塔天神), the god who received hospitality from Somin Shōrai, is identified with [[Susanoo]] (pictured)]] The earliest version of the Somin Shōrai legend is found in the imperially commissioned gazetteer (''[[Fudoki]]'') of [[Bingo Province]] (modern eastern [[Hiroshima Prefecture]]) compiled during the [[Nara period]], surviving in an extract quoted by medieval scholar and Shinto priest Urabe Kanekata in the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. [[File:Gozu-Tenno.jpg|thumb|left|Gozu Tennō, a [[Shinbutsu shūgō|syncretic]] deity associated with plague and disease historically conflated with Susanoo and Mutō-no-Kami]] According to the story, a god from the northern sea named, ''Mutō-no-Kami'' (武塔神), who was embarking on a long journey to court the daughter of his counterpart, the god of the southern sea, asked for a place to stay for the night from two brothers. While the wealthy, younger brother refused the deity any hospitality, the elder brother, named ''Somin Shōrai'', though extremely poor, took him in as a guest and shared all that he had: a bed of [[Foxtail millet|millet]]-straw and a simple meal of cooked millet seeds. Years later, ''Mutō'' (who had since married the southern sea god's daughter and had eight children with her) returned to ''Somin Shōrai's'' old house and gave to ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter a [[wreath]] of entwined [[miscanthus]] reeds (a [[Shimenawa]] wreath) as a reward for the hospitality shown to him by her late father, telling her to wear it around her waist from then-on. At that same night, the god exterminated all the inhabitants of the area, sparing only ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter. ''Mutō'' then revealed himself to have actually been the god, ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'', and promised to her that, in the future, ''all'' those who would wear miscanthus wreaths around their waists while declaring themselves to be ''Somin Shōrai's'' descendants, in times of epidemics, would remain unharmed.{{cite book |editor1-last=Akimoto |editor1-first=Kichirō |title=日本古典文学大系 2 風土記 (Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei, 2: Fudoki) |date=1958 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=488–489}}{{cite journal |last1=McMullin |first1=Neil |title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult |journal=History of Religions |date=February 1988 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=270–293 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press|doi=10.1086/463123 |jstor=1062279 |s2cid=162357693 }} In some later versions of the legend, the rich younger brother is given the name ''Kotan Shōrai'' (巨旦将来), while the god who stayed in ''Somin Shōrai's'' house is identified, instead of Susanoo, as ''[[Gozu Tennō]]'' (牛頭天王 'Ox-Headed Heavenly King'), a pestilence deity conflated with both ''Mutō'' and ''Susanoo'' (though one version instead identifies ''Gozu Tennō'' as ''Mutō's'' son).{{cite book |title=The Japan Mail |date=1878-03-11 |publisher=Jappan Mēru Shinbunsha |pages=138–139 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IhJCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA169}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |page=181 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA181}}{{cite web |title=牛頭天王と蘇民将来 (Gozu Tennō to Somin Shōrai) |url=http://www.futami-somin.com/somin.htm |website=民話の駅 蘇民 (Minwa no Eki Somin) |access-date=2020-06-17 |language=ja}} In the version of the story found in the ''[[:ja:三国相伝陰陽カン轄ホキ内伝金烏玉兎集|Hoki Naiden]]'', an ''[[Onmyōdō]]'' text on divination attributed to ''[[Abe no Seimei]]'', ''Kotan Shōrai'' (portrayed here as the king of an Indian kingdom), who was slain by ''Gozu Tennō'' as punishment for his lack of generosity, is identified with the evil deity, ''[[Konjin]]''. In other variants, the divine traveller gives ''Somin Shōrai's'' family a talisman with the inscription "[I am] the descendant of ''Somin Shōrai''" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') as protection against pestilence. ==''Chi-no-wa kuguri''== [[File:Haiden of Kamado Shrine.jpg|thumb|290px|A ''chi-no-wa'' erected in front of a Shinto shrine]] The legend forms the basis for the ''chi-no-wa kuguri'' (茅の輪くぐり) ritual performed in many [[Shinto shrines]] mainly during the annual Summer Purification Ritual (夏越の祓 ''Nagoshi no [[Harae]]'' or 夏越の大祓 ''Nagoshi no Ōharae'') held at the end of June. This rite involves passing through a large miscanthus hoop (茅の輪 ''chi-no-wa'') set up at the shrine's entrance or within its precincts, usually while reciting one or more seasonal ''[[Waka (poetry)|waka]]'' such as the one below followed by a double recitation of Somin Shōrai's name. Walking through this ring is believed to cleanse sins and other defilements (''[[kegare]]'') and guard against misfortune.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Nagoshi no harae |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1064 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |last1=Motosawa |first1=Masashi |title=Chinowa |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=301 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=Nagoshi Gohan |url=https://kome-academy.com/en/teishoku_library/season/2018_summer.html |website=Plenus "kome" Academy |access-date=2020-06-20}}{{cite web |title=「茅の輪くぐり」唱え詞 |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/%E3%80%8C%E8%8C%85%E3%81%AE%E8%BC%AA%E3%81%8F%E3%81%90%E3%82%8A%E3%80%8D%E5%94%B1%E3%81%88%E8%A9%9E/ |website=諫早神社 (Isahaya Shrine) |access-date=2020-06-20 |language=ja}} {{Verse translation | lang = Japanese | italicsoff = 1 | rtl1 = |水無月の (''Minazuki no'')
夏越の祓 (''Nagoshi no harae'')
する人は (''suru hito wa'')
千歳の命 (''chitose no inochi'')
延ぶといふなり (''nobu to iu nari'') |In [[Japanese calendar#Months|Minazuki]],
at the passing of summer,
those who purify themselves
are said to have a thousand years
added to their lifespan. | attr1 = Anonymous, ''[[Shūi Wakashū]]'' | attr2 = }} ==Worship== [[File:Sominshorai.jpg|thumb|120px|Wooden charm with Somin Shōrai's name]] Somin Shōrai is venerated in a few shrines as a deity against disease, such as in Eki Shrine (疫神社 ''Eki-jinja'', lit. 'Plague Shrine'), an auxiliary shrine within the precincts of [[Yasaka Shrine]] in the [[Gion]] District of [[Kyoto]].{{cite web|url=http://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/map.html|title=境内マップ|website=Yasaka Shrine|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} The story is also the inspiration for the Somin Festival (蘇民祭 ''Somin-sai'') held in various places within [[Iwate Prefecture]], the most famous of which being the one held every February at [[:ja:黒石寺|Kokuseki-ji Temple]] in [[Ōshū, Iwate|Ōshū City]]. In this festival, hundreds of [[Hadaka Matsuri|half-naked men]] compete to grab the 'Somin bag' (蘇民袋 ''Somin-bukuro''), which is said to bring good fortune.{{cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/articles/iwate-hayachine-shrine-somin-festival|title=Sominsai at Hayachine Shrine (早池峰神社蘇民祭) Hanamaki City, Iwate Pref.|website=OH! MATSURi|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://en.japantravel.com/iwate/kokusekiji-sominsai-in-oshuu/57118|title=Kokusekiji Sominsai in Oshuu|website=JapanTravel|date=18 February 2019 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://www.kingdom-of-winter-trip-tohoku.jp/spot/en/63.html|title=Kokusekiji Temple Somin-sai Festival|website=Kingdom of Winter Trip TOHOKU|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==Amulets== [[File:Somin shourai kamon.jpg|thumb|left|230px|A ''[[shimenawa]]'' above a doorway with a plaque bearing the inscription "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon'')]] Various types of amulets (''[[omamori]]'') bearing Somin Shōrai's name is distributed by a number of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples across Japan. In Kyoto, a talisman resembling a ''chimaki'' (a [[Zongzi|rice dumpling]] wrapped in bamboo leaves) on which is attached a slip of paper with the words "The descendant(s) of Somin Shōrai" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') is traditionally hung on doorways to ward off misfortune.{{cite web|url=http://eng.trip.kyoto.jp/spot/db/onchimakishi-kawabatadouki/|title=Onchimakishi Kawabata Doki|website=Today, KYOTO, Tomorrow|date=27 June 2013 |access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=https://tadaimajp.com/2016/10/chimaki_en/|title=You'll often see a chimaki at the front door of private houses in Kyoto. This is actually considered an amulet.|website=Tadaima Japan|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ashikariyama.jp/chimaki-e.html|title=About Chimaki|website=Foundation for Ashikari-yama Preservation|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite book |title=Transactions and Proceedings of the Japan Society, London, vol. 12|editor=Japan Society of London|date=1914|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner and Company|pages=69–70|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=B_EVAAAAYAAJ}} A similar custom, involving a ''[[shimenawa]]'' with a wooden plaque on which is written "The house of Somin Shōrai's descendants" (蘇民将来子孫家門, ''Somin Shōrai shison kamon''), is practiced in the [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Shima, Mie|Shima]] areas of [[Mie Prefecture]].{{cite book |title=Iseshima Pedia: Guide to Iseshima: Mie Japan|date=2016|publisher=Iseshima Tourism & Convention Organization|page=35|url=https://www.iseshima-kanko.jp/kiji/iseshimapedia/Iseshima_Pedia_lite.pdf}} In [[Ueda, Nagano|Ueda]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], hexagonal wooden charms inscribed with the words "Wealthy [and] prosperous [are] Somin Shōrai's descendant(s)" (大福長者蘇民将来子孫人也, ''Daifuku chōja Somin Shōrai shison no hito nari'') traditionally handmade by members of the local Somin Confraternity (蘇民講 ''Somin-kō'') are distributed by [[Shinano Kokubun-ji]] temple during the Yōkadō Festival (八日堂縁日 ''Yōkadō Ennichi'') held every 7th-8 January.{{cite web|url=http://bunkashisan.ne.jp/search/ViewContent_e.php?from=10&ContentID=262|title=Somin Shorai-fu or Somin Shorai talisman - Our wish is forever and ever - Folkways-cultural record of the distribution of Somin Shorai-fu of Yokado, Ueda City|website=Regional Cultural Asset Portal|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://shinano-kokubunji.or.jp/youkadou.html|title=八日堂縁日について|website=Shinano Kokubun-ji|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}}{{cite web|url=http://db.umic.ueda.nagano.jp/johogura/kangawa/0527045/0527045e.html|title=Somin Shorai Talisman Makers Shinano Kokubunji Temple|website= Ueda City Multimedia Information Center|language=ja|access-date=2020-09-16}} ==See also== {{Portal|Mythology|Religion|Philosophy}} * [[Baucis and Philemon]] * [[Folk medicine]] * [[Harae]] * [[Omamori]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Sodom and Gomorrah]] * [[Susanoo]] * [[Xenia (Greek)|Theoxenia]] ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Somin Shourai}} *[http://live.ueda.ne.jp/kokubu.html Shinano Kokubunji Temple] {{Gion cult}} {{citation needed|Somin Shōrai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Woodcarving]] [[Category:Onmyōdō deities]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Somin Shōrai}}

{{short description|Figure in Japanese folklore}} In [[Japanese mythology]] and [[Japanese folklore|folklore]], '''Somin Shōrai''' (蘇民将来, ''[[kyūjitai]]'': 蘇民將來; also [[Variant Chinese character|written as]] 蘓民將耒) was a poor man who gave food and shelter to a certain [[Kami|god]] in the guise of a traveler who was looking for a place to stay. As a reward, the god provided Somin Shōrai's family a means to save themselves from an oncoming pestilence that eventually claimed the lives of those who had turned him away earlier. The story of Somin Shōrai is the basis for the [[Shinto]] custom of walking through a large ring of twisted [[miscanthus]] reeds during the beginning of summer at many [[Shinto shrine]]s across Japan. [[Omamori|Talismans]] bearing Somin Shōrai's name are also popularly held to ward off disease and misfortune. ==Legend== [[File:Meiji standing screen from Crow Collection 02.jpg|thumb|Mutō-no-Kami (武塔神) or Mutō Tenjin (武塔天神), the god who received hospitality from Somin Shōrai, is identified with [[Susanoo]] (pictured)]] The earliest version of the Somin Shōrai legend is found in the imperially commissioned gazetteer (''[[Fudoki]]'') of [[Bingo Province]] (modern eastern [[Hiroshima Prefecture]]) compiled during the [[Nara period]], surviving in an extract quoted by medieval scholar and Shinto priest Urabe Kanekata in the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. [[File:Gozu-Tenno.jpg|thumb|left|Gozu Tennō, a [[Shinbutsu shūgō|syncretic]] deity associated with plague and disease historically conflated with Susanoo and Mutō-no-Kami]] According to the story, a god from the northern sea named, ''Mutō-no-Kami'' (武塔神), who was embarking on a long journey to court the daughter of his counterpart, the god of the southern sea, asked for a place to stay for the night from two brothers. While the wealthy, younger brother refused the deity any hospitality, the elder brother, named ''Somin Shōrai'', though extremely poor, took him in as a guest and shared all that he had: a bed of [[Foxtail millet|millet]]-straw and a simple meal of cooked millet seeds. Years later, ''Mutō'' (who had since married the southern sea god's daughter and had eight children with her) returned to ''Somin Shōrai's'' old house and gave to ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter a [[wreath]] of entwined [[miscanthus]] reeds (a [[Shimenawa]] wreath) as a reward for the hospitality shown to him by her late father, telling her to wear it around her waist from then-on. At that same night, the god exterminated all the inhabitants of the area, sparing only ''Somin Shōrai's'' daughter. ''Mutō'' then revealed himself to have actually been the god, ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'', and promised to her that, in the future, ''all'' those who would wear miscanthus wreaths around their waists while declaring themselves to be ''Somin Shōrai's'' descendants, in times of epidemics, would remain unharmed.{{cite book |editor1-last=Akimoto |editor1-first=Kichirō |title=日本古典文学大系 2 風土記 (Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei, 2: Fudoki) |date=1958 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=488–489}}{{cite journal |last1=McMullin |first1=Neil |title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult |journal=History of Religions |date=February 1988 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=270–293 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press|doi=10.1086/463123 |jstor=1062279 |s2cid=162357693 }} In some later versions of the legend, the rich younger brother is given the name ''Kotan Shōrai'' (巨旦将来), while the god who stayed in ''Somin Shōrai's'' house is identified, instead of Susanoo, as ''[[Gozu Tennō]]'' (牛頭天王 'Ox-Headed Heavenly King'), a pestilence deity conflated with both ''Mutō'' and ''Susanoo'' (though one version instead identifies ''Gozu Tennō'' as ''Mutō's'' son).{{cite book |title=The Japan Mail |date=1878-03-11 |publisher=Jappan Mēru Shinbunsha |pages=138–139 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IhJCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA169}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |page=181 |isbn=9780190621711 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&pg=PA181}}{{cite web |title=牛頭天王と蘇民将来 (Gozu Tennō to Somin Shōrai) |url=http://www.futami-somin.com/somin.htm |website=民話の駅 蘇民 (Minwa no Eki Somin) |access-date=2020-06-17 |language=ja}} In the version of the story found in the ''[[:ja:三国相伝陰陽カン轄ホキ内伝金烏玉兎集|Hoki Naiden]]'', an ''[[Onmyōdō]]'' text on divination attributed to ''[[Abe no Seimei]]'', ''Kotan Shōrai'' (portrayed here as the king of an Indian kingdom), who was slain by ''Gozu Tennō'' as punishment for his lack of generosity, is identified with the evil deity, ''[[Konjin]]''. In other variants, the divine traveller gives ''Somin Shōrai's'' family a talisman with the inscription "[I am] the descendant of ''Somin Shōrai''" (蘇民将来之子孫也, ''Somin Shōrai no shison nari'') as protection against pestilence. ==''Chi-no-wa kuguri''== [[File:Haiden of Kamado Shrine.jpg|thumb|290px|A ''chi-no-wa'' erected in front of a Shinto shrine]] The legend forms the basis for the ''chi-no-wa kuguri'' (茅の輪くぐり) ritual performed in many [[Shinto shrines]] mainly during the annual Summer Purification Ritual (夏越の祓 ''Nagoshi no [[Harae]]'' or 夏越の大祓 ''Nagoshi no Ōharae'') held at the end of June.
